two branches of stay test O'Connor Babos

subject: public_law
chapter_title: Charter of Rights and Freedoms — Section 1 and Key Rights
topic_heading: Charter Rights — Section 24(2) Exclusion of Evidence
subtopic_heading: s. 24(2) — Grant Test for Exclusion of Evidence
fact_pattern: Police conduct an unlawful search of a vehicle without reasonable grounds, violating s. 8 of the Charter. They discover a kilogram of cocaine in the trunk. The accused applies to exclude the cocaine under s. 24(2) of the Charter, arguing its admission would bring the administration of justice into disrepute. The Crown argues the evidence is reliable and should be admitted despite the breach.
call_of_question: Under s. 24(2) of the Charter and the Grant test, what three inquiries must a court conduct to determine whether to exclude evidence obtained in breach of the Charter?

options.A: The court must determine whether the evidence is reliable, relevant, and not prejudicial; if all three are satisfied, the evidence is admitted.
options.B: The court must determine: (1) the seriousness of the Charter-infringing state conduct; (2) the impact of the breach on the accused's Charter-protected interests; and (3) society's interest in adjudication on the merits. Evidence must be excluded if admission would bring the administration of justice into disrepute.
options.C: Under s. 24(2), all evidence obtained through a Charter breach is automatically excluded; no balancing test applies.
options.D: Evidence obtained through a Charter breach is excluded only if the breach was deliberate and intentional; accidental or good-faith breaches always allow admission.

explanation: In R v Grant, 2009 SCC 32, the Supreme Court of Canada replaced the older Collins/Stillman framework with the three-part Grant test for s. 24(2). The court must assess: (1) the seriousness of the Charter-infringing state conduct — deliberate, flagrant breaches weigh more heavily toward exclusion; (2) the impact of the breach on the accused's Charter-protected interests — more serious intrusions into reasonable expectations of privacy weigh toward exclusion; and (3) society's interest in adjudication on the merits — reliable physical evidence in serious cases weighs toward admission. The court balances all three and excludes the evidence only if admission would bring the administration of justice into disrepute.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The Grant test under s. 24(2) is not about reliability, relevance, and prejudice in the conventional evidentiary sense; it is a public confidence in justice analysis involving three specific Charter-related inquiries.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The court must determine: (1) the seriousness of the Charter-infringing state conduct; (2) the impact of the breach on the accused's Charter-protected interests; and (3) society's interest in adjudication on the merits. Evidence must be excluded if admission would bring the administration of justice into disrepute.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Section 24(2) provides for discretionary exclusion, not automatic exclusion; the Grant test requires balancing three inquiries and excludes evidence only if admission would bring the administration of justice into disrepute.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Good faith conduct by police is relevant to the first Grant inquiry (seriousness of the breach) and weighs against exclusion, but it is not an automatic rule admitting all evidence from good-faith breaches; the full three-part analysis still applies.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.heading: s. 24(2) — Grant Test for Exclusion of Evidence
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 24(2); R v Grant, 2009 SCC 32

tested_concepts: seriousness of breach impact on accused society's interest

subject: public_law
chapter_title: Charter of Rights and Freedoms — Section 1 and Key Rights
topic_heading: Charter Rights — Section 24(2) Exclusion of Evidence
subtopic_heading: s. 24(2) — Application in Non-Criminal Proceedings
fact_pattern: A regulatory tribunal investigating a professional's conduct obtains documents from the professional's office through an administrative inspection conducted without proper authorization, violating the professional's s. 8 Charter rights. The professional applies to exclude the documents from the regulatory hearing under s. 24(2) of the Charter, arguing the Grant test requires exclusion.
call_of_question: Does s. 24(2) of the Charter and the Grant test apply to the exclusion of evidence in civil or administrative regulatory proceedings?

options.A: Yes. Section 24(2) and the Grant test apply equally to all proceedings — criminal, civil, and administrative — wherever a Charter breach occurs.
options.B: Section 24(2) by its terms applies only where evidence is sought to be used in proceedings against the accused in whose respect the breach occurred; courts have held that the Grant test is designed for criminal proceedings and that a different — typically broader — framework applies to exclusion of evidence in civil and administrative proceedings, where the focus is on the fairness of the proceeding rather than the Grant three-part balancing.
options.C: Section 24(2) never applies in administrative proceedings; all evidence obtained through any means is admissible before regulatory tribunals.
options.D: Section 24(2) applies in administrative proceedings only if the tribunal's enabling statute expressly incorporates it.

explanation: Section 24(2) of the Charter provides that where evidence is obtained in a manner that infringed or denied any rights or freedoms guaranteed by the Charter, it may be excluded 'if it is established that, having regard to all the circumstances, the admission of it in the proceedings would bring the administration of justice into disrepute.' While s. 24(2) technically applies to any 'proceedings,' the Grant test was developed in the criminal context. Courts have held that the Grant framework is specifically calibrated for criminal proceedings; in civil and administrative contexts, evidence exclusion is governed by broader fairness principles. Tribunals have some discretion to exclude improperly obtained evidence on fairness grounds, but the Grant three-part balancing test does not directly transplant into regulatory proceedings.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. While s. 24(2) may technically apply in non-criminal proceedings, the Grant test was developed specifically for criminal proceedings; it does not apply with the same force in civil or administrative settings where different considerations govern fairness.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 24(2) by its terms applies only where evidence is sought to be used in proceedings against the accused in whose respect the breach occurred; courts have held that the Grant test is designed for criminal proceedings and that a different — typically broader — framework applies to exclusion in civil and administrative proceedings, where the focus is on the fairness of the proceeding rather than the Grant three-part balancing.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Regulatory tribunals are not free to admit all evidence regardless of how it was obtained; fairness considerations — grounded in common law and the Charter — may justify exclusion in appropriate cases.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. The applicability of Charter remedies is a constitutional matter; it does not depend on whether the enabling statute incorporates s. 24(2) by reference.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.heading: s. 24(2) — Application in Non-Criminal Proceedings
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 24(2); R v Grant, 2009 SCC 32

fairness grounds for exclusion in regulatory hearings

subject: public_law
chapter_title: Charter of Rights and Freedoms — Section 1 and Key Rights
topic_heading: Charter Rights — Section 8 Search and Seizure
subtopic_heading: s. 8 — Reasonable Expectation of Privacy — Informational Privacy
fact_pattern: Police, without a warrant, send a production order to a person's cell phone provider requiring disclosure of all text messages sent and received over a six-month period. The messages were stored on the provider's servers. The accused argues this constitutes an unreasonable search under s. 8 of the Charter because she had a reasonable expectation of privacy in the content of her private messages.
call_of_question: Under R v Marakah and the s. 8 reasonable expectation of privacy analysis, does a person retain a reasonable expectation of privacy in the content of text messages stored on a third party's servers?

options.A: No. Once messages are transmitted and stored on a third party's server, the sender loses all reasonable expectation of privacy in their content.
options.B: Yes. Under R v Marakah, 2017 SCC 59, a person retains a reasonable expectation of privacy in the content of private text messages even after they are stored on a third-party server; the content of private communications reflects the sender's core biographical information and attracts strong s. 8 protection.
options.C: No. A production order — unlike a search warrant — does not constitute a 'search' within the meaning of s. 8.
options.D: Yes, but only if the messages have not been deleted from the sender's phone.

explanation: In R v Marakah, 2017 SCC 59, the Supreme Court of Canada held that individuals may retain a reasonable expectation of privacy in text messages even after they are sent and stored on a third-party server. The analysis uses the totality-of-circumstances approach: does the claimant have a subjective expectation of privacy, and is that expectation objectively reasonable? The Court held that the content of private communications reveals biographical core information — the kinds of details about one's personal life that attract the highest s. 8 protection. A warrantless production order compelling disclosure of private text messages from a cell phone provider therefore constitutes an unreasonable search in violation of s. 8.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The third-party doctrine — that sending information to a third party eliminates all privacy — was qualified by Marakah; the Court held that privacy in communications content survives even after transmission and storage.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Yes. Under R v Marakah, 2017 SCC 59, a person retains a reasonable expectation of privacy in the content of private text messages even after they are stored on a third-party server; the content of private communications reflects the sender's core biographical information and attracts strong s. 8 protection.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. A production order that compels disclosure of private communications constitutes a search within the meaning of s. 8; the form of the compulsory process does not determine whether there is a search.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. The reasonable expectation of privacy in the content of messages is not contingent on whether the original message remains on the sender's phone; Marakah addressed precisely this scenario of third-party server storage.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.heading: s. 8 — Reasonable Expectation of Privacy — Informational Privacy
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 8; R v Marakah, 2017 SCC 59

third-party server storage does not eliminate privacy
biographical core information highest s. 8 protection

subject: public_law
chapter_title: Charter of Rights and Freedoms — Section 1 and Key Rights
topic_heading: Charter Rights — Section 8 Search and Seizure
subtopic_heading: s. 8 — Search of One's Home — Heightened Expectation of Privacy
fact_pattern: Police receive a tip that a person is growing cannabis at his home. Without obtaining a search warrant, they enter the property and look through the windows into the house. They smell cannabis and see equipment through a window. Using this observation, they obtain a warrant and conduct a formal search, finding a large grow operation. The accused argues that the initial warrantless entry onto his property to peer through windows violated s. 8 of the Charter.
call_of_question: Does a person's home attract the highest level of reasonable expectation of privacy under s. 8 of the Charter, and can police approach a private dwelling to observe through windows without engaging s. 8?

options.A: No. Once police are on public streets, they may look through any window visible from the street without engaging s. 8.
options.B: Yes. Under Canadian constitutional law, a person's home is the location where the reasonable expectation of privacy is highest; police entry onto the curtilage of a home (the area immediately surrounding the dwelling) to conduct observations is a search engaging s. 8, even if no physical entry into the structure occurs.
options.C: No. Observation through a window is not a 'search' because the officer does not physically touch or enter the property.
options.D: Yes, but only if the windows have blinds or curtains drawn; windows left open to view from the street attract no privacy.

explanation: The Supreme Court of Canada has consistently held that a person's home is the location of the highest reasonable expectation of privacy. The concept of 'curtilage' — the area immediately surrounding a dwelling — is also protected. In R v Evans, [1996] 1 SCR 8, the Court held that approaching a private home in a manner inconsistent with an implied licence (such as by going to the back of the house to peer through windows rather than approaching the front door) constitutes a trespass that engages s. 8. Police cannot circumvent the warrant requirement by conducting clandestine observations from the curtilage of a private home.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The fact that the police are approaching from a public area does not justify entering the curtilage of a home to make covert observations; the implied licence to approach a home extends only to the front door, not to circumnavigating the property to observe.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Yes. A person's home is the location where the reasonable expectation of privacy is highest; police entry onto the curtilage of a home to conduct observations is a search engaging s. 8, even if no physical entry into the structure occurs.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. A search under s. 8 is not defined by physical touching; it encompasses any state act that interferes with a person's reasonable expectation of privacy, including surveillance of the home through its windows from the curtilage.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. The absence of blinds or curtains does not eliminate a reasonable expectation of privacy in one's home; privacy expectations in the home are not forfeited simply because some visibility exists from the outside.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.heading: s. 8 — Search of One's Home — Heightened Expectation of Privacy
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 8; R v Evans, [1996] 1 SCR 8

tested_concepts: home as location of highest privacy expectation
police observations from curtilage constitute a search

subject: public_law
chapter_title: Charter of Rights and Freedoms — Section 1 and Key Rights
topic_heading: Charter Rights — Section 12 Cruel and Unusual Treatment
subtopic_heading: s. 12 — Reasonable Hypothetical Approach for Mandatory Minimums
fact_pattern: Parliament enacts a mandatory minimum sentence of five years for anyone convicted of firearms trafficking. An offender who sold a single firearm to one person — with no violence, no criminal history, and strong mitigating personal circumstances — is convicted and sentenced to the mandatory minimum. Her counsel argues the mandatory minimum is grossly disproportionate even if some traffickers deserve five years. The Crown argues the sentence is not disproportionate for the worst case scenario.
call_of_question: Under the s. 12 'reasonable hypothetical' approach established in R v Nur and R v Lloyd, must a mandatory minimum sentence be grossly disproportionate as applied to the actual accused before the court, or may it be challenged on the basis that it would be grossly disproportionate as applied to a reasonably foreseeable offender?

options.A: The s. 12 challenge must be based on the actual accused before the court; a mandatory minimum cannot be challenged based on hypothetical situations.
options.B: A mandatory minimum may be challenged as grossly disproportionate as applied to a reasonably foreseeable offender who would be caught by the provision — not just the actual accused. If the mandatory minimum would constitute cruel and unusual punishment for such a reasonably foreseeable offender, the provision is unconstitutional.
options.C: A mandatory minimum is unconstitutional under s. 12 only if it is grossly disproportionate for every possible offender to whom it could apply.
options.D: Courts may never challenge mandatory minimums under s. 12; sentencing policy is exclusively within Parliament's domain.

explanation: In R v Nur, 2015 SCC 15, and R v Lloyd, 2016 SCC 13, the Supreme Court of Canada confirmed the reasonable hypothetical approach to s. 12 challenges to mandatory minimums. The court first considers whether the sentence is grossly disproportionate for the actual offender. If not, it considers whether a reasonable hypothetical offender at the low end of the spectrum of conduct captured by the provision would receive a grossly disproportionate sentence. If so, the mandatory minimum violates s. 12. This approach recognizes that mandatory minimums operate across a range of offenders; the constitutional test is whether any reasonably foreseeable application would be grossly disproportionate, not whether it is disproportionate for every offender.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Nur and Lloyd specifically confirmed that the reasonable hypothetical approach allows courts to consider whether the mandatory minimum would be grossly disproportionate for foreseeable offenders other than the actual accused.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. A mandatory minimum may be challenged as grossly disproportionate as applied to a reasonably foreseeable offender who would be caught by the provision — not just the actual accused. If the mandatory minimum would constitute cruel and unusual punishment for such a reasonably foreseeable offender, the provision is unconstitutional.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The test does not require gross disproportionality for every possible offender; it is satisfied if the mandatory minimum is grossly disproportionate for a reasonably foreseeable offender at the low end of the spectrum.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. The Charter, including s. 12, applies to Parliament's sentencing legislation; the Supreme Court has consistently reviewed mandatory minimums under s. 12 and struck down those that constitute cruel and unusual punishment.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.heading: s. 12 — Reasonable Hypothetical Approach for Mandatory Minimums
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 12; R v Nur, 2015 SCC 15; R v Lloyd, 2016 SCC 13

tested_concepts: reasonable hypothetical approach to s. 12 challenge
mandatory minimum unconstitutional if disproportionate for foreseeable offender

subject: public_law
chapter_title: Charter of Rights and Freedoms — Section 1 and Key Rights
topic_heading: Charter Rights — Section 15 Equality Rights
subtopic_heading: s. 15 — Substantive Equality and Perpetuation of Disadvantage
fact_pattern: A federal law provides social assistance benefits that are set at a lower rate for persons with disabilities who can work part-time versus those who cannot work at all. A claimant with a disability who can work part-time receives lower benefits despite having significantly higher disability-related expenses. She argues the distinction perpetuates the historical disadvantage of persons with disabilities.
call_of_question: Under the substantive equality approach to s. 15 of the Charter, why is formal equality — treating similarly situated people the same — insufficient to avoid a s. 15 violation?

options.A: Formal equality is sufficient; as long as the government applies the same rules to everyone in the same category, s. 15 is satisfied.
options.B: Under the substantive equality approach affirmed in Withler v Canada and Fraser v Canada, s. 15 demands that laws recognize and accommodate the actual circumstances, needs, and disadvantages of claimants — not just that they be treated identically to others. A law that treats different groups in a formally neutral way may nonetheless perpetuate disadvantage if it fails to account for material differences in circumstances.
options.C: Substantive equality means every person must receive the same dollar amount of benefits regardless of individual circumstances.
options.D: Substantive equality is satisfied as long as the law was enacted with good intentions toward disadvantaged groups.

explanation: The Supreme Court of Canada adopted a substantive equality approach to s. 15 in Andrews v Law Society of BC, [1989] 1 SCR 143, and developed it through Withler v Canada (Attorney General), 2011 SCC 12, and Fraser v Canada (Attorney General), 2020 SCC 28. Substantive equality recognizes that treating unequal situations the same — formal equality — can itself be discriminatory where it fails to account for real differences in circumstances. The claimant's higher disability-related costs mean that lower benefit rates may impose a disproportionate burden on the group to which she belongs, potentially perpetuating the historical disadvantage of persons with disabilities and violating s. 15.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The Supreme Court has expressly rejected formal equality as the standard for s. 15; Andrews and subsequent cases established that substantive equality requires accounting for actual circumstances, needs, and disadvantages.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under the substantive equality approach affirmed in Withler v Canada and Fraser v Canada, s. 15 demands that laws recognize and accommodate the actual circumstances, needs, and disadvantages of claimants — not just that they be treated identically to others. A law that treats different groups in a formally neutral way may nonetheless perpetuate disadvantage if it fails to account for material differences in circumstances.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Substantive equality does not require identical dollar amounts; it requires recognition of and accommodation for actual differences in circumstances and needs.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Legislative intent does not determine whether a law perpetuates disadvantage; s. 15 is effects-based — the impact of the law on the claimant's group is what matters, not the government's motive.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.heading: s. 15 — Substantive Equality and Perpetuation of Disadvantage
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 15; Andrews v Law Society of BC, [1989] 1 SCR 143; Withler v Canada (Attorney General), 2011 SCC 12; Fraser v Canada (Attorney General), 2020 SCC 28

subject: public_law
chapter_title: Charter of Rights and Freedoms — Section 1 and Key Rights
topic_heading: Charter Rights — Section 1 Oakes Proportionality of Effects
subtopic_heading: s. 1 — Proportionality of Effects (Stage 2(c) of Oakes)
fact_pattern: Parliament enacts legislation banning all outdoor advertising by tobacco companies. The government establishes that the ban has a pressing and substantial objective (reducing tobacco use), is rationally connected to that goal, and represents the minimum impairment possible to achieve the objective. The tobacco companies argue the final step of the Oakes test — proportionality of effects — is not satisfied because the total prohibition is devastating to their business.
call_of_question: What does the 'proportionality of effects' component (Stage 2(c)) of the Oakes test require the government to demonstrate?

options.A: Stage 2(c) requires the government to prove that the law will succeed in eliminating the targeted harm entirely.
options.B: Stage 2(c) requires a final overall balancing: the benefits of the limitation to the public must be proportionate to the severity of the rights violation; a law that achieves a significant public benefit may satisfy this stage even if the limitation on the right is substantial, provided the overall balance is proportionate.
options.C: Stage 2(c) is automatically satisfied once minimal impairment is established; there is no separate effects test.
options.D: Stage 2(c) requires the government to compensate all parties whose rights were limited by the legislation.

explanation: Stage 2(c) of the Oakes test — proportionality of effects — requires the court to conduct a final overall balancing between the benefits of the limitation and the severity of its effects on the right infringed. Even where a law is minimally impairing (Stage 2(b)), the court must ask whether the benefits conferred by the law — measured by the importance of the objective achieved — outweigh the actual limitation imposed on the right. In RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1995] 3 SCR 199, the Supreme Court found that even a total advertising ban may satisfy Stage 2(c) if the benefits to public health outweigh the impact on freedom of expression of tobacco companies, which occupy a lower protected status given the harm of the product. The final balance is a proportionality assessment, not a mathematical calculation.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Stage 2(c) does not require proof of complete success in eliminating the harm; it requires an overall proportionality balance between benefits and the severity of the rights limitation.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Stage 2(c) requires a final overall balancing: the benefits of the limitation to the public must be proportionate to the severity of the rights violation; a law that achieves a significant public benefit may satisfy this stage even if the limitation on the right is substantial, provided the overall balance is proportionate.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Stage 2(c) is a separate component of the proportionality analysis; it is not automatically subsumed into minimal impairment. Courts have applied Stage 2(c) as an independent requirement even after finding minimal impairment was satisfied.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Section 1 does not require the government to compensate rights-holders; successful justification under s. 1 means the limitation is valid and no remedy — including compensation — is required.

source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 1; R v Oakes, [1986] 1 SCR 103; RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1995] 3 SCR 199

subject: public_law
chapter_title: Charter of Rights and Freedoms — Section 1 and Key Rights
topic_heading: Charter Rights — Section 6 Mobility Rights
subtopic_heading: s. 6 — Mobility Rights — Right to Move and Take Up Residence
fact_pattern: A professional engineer moves from Ontario to British Columbia seeking employment. British Columbia requires all engineers practising in the province to hold a B.C. licence. The engineer argues that the provincial licensing requirement infringes her mobility right to pursue gainful employment anywhere in Canada under s. 6(2)(b) of the Charter. British Columbia argues that its licensing requirement is a reasonable professional standard, not a violation of mobility rights.
call_of_question: Under s. 6(2)(b) of the Charter, what is the scope of the right of Canadian citizens and permanent residents to pursue gainful employment in any province?

options.A: Section 6(2)(b) gives every Canadian citizen and permanent resident an absolute right to work in any profession in any province without meeting any provincial requirements.
options.B: Section 6(2)(b) guarantees the right of Canadian citizens and permanent residents to pursue the gaining of a livelihood in any province; however, under s. 6(3)(a), provincial laws of general application that do not discriminate against persons on the basis of their previous province of residence are not affected by s. 6(2). A B.C. licensing requirement that applies equally to all persons — including B.C. residents — does not violate s. 6(2)(b).
options.C: Section 6(2)(b) applies only to interprovincial movement; once a person has moved to a province and taken up residence, their rights under s. 6(2)(b) are spent.
options.D: Section 6(2)(b) is subject to s. 33; provinces may use the notwithstanding clause to override mobility rights.

explanation: Section 6(2)(b) of the Charter guarantees that every citizen of Canada and every person who has the status of a permanent resident of Canada has the right to pursue the gaining of a livelihood in any province. However, s. 6(3)(a) provides that ss. 6(2)(a) and (b) do not preclude any law or practice of general application in force in a province unless it is intended to, or has the effect of, discriminating against persons on the basis of their previous province of residence. A professional licensing requirement that applies equally to all persons seeking to practise engineering in B.C. — regardless of where they previously resided — does not violate s. 6(2)(b) because it does not discriminate on the basis of previous province of residence. Section 6 is not in the group of rights subject to s. 33.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Section 6(2)(b) does not provide an absolute right to work in any profession without meeting any requirements; s. 6(3)(a) specifically preserves laws of general application that do not discriminate based on prior province of residence.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 6(2)(b) guarantees the right to pursue gainful employment in any province; however, under s. 6(3)(a), provincial laws of general application that do not discriminate on the basis of previous province of residence are preserved. A B.C. licensing requirement applying equally to all does not violate s. 6(2)(b).
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The right to pursue a livelihood under s. 6(2)(b) is not exhausted by moving to a province; it is an ongoing right to work anywhere in Canada without discrimination based on prior provincial residence.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Section 6 mobility rights are expressly outside the scope of s. 33 notwithstanding clause; provinces cannot override mobility rights through a s. 33 declaration.

source_reference.heading: s. 6 — Mobility Rights — Right to Move and Take Up Residence
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 6(2)(b), s. 6(3)(a)

s. 6(3)(a) exception for laws of general application
mobility rights cannot be overridden by s. 33

subject: public_law
chapter_title: Charter of Rights and Freedoms — Section 1 and Key Rights
topic_heading: Charter Rights — Section 3 Democratic Rights
subtopic_heading: s. 3 — Right to Vote and Right to Stand for Election
fact_pattern: A provincial law disqualifies persons who have been convicted of a criminal offence and are currently serving a sentence of imprisonment from voting in provincial elections. An imprisoned person challenges the law as a violation of his right to vote under s. 3 of the Charter. The government argues the law is justified under s. 33.
call_of_question: Can a provincial legislature use the s. 33 notwithstanding clause to override s. 3 democratic rights to disenfranchise prisoners?

options.A: Yes. A provincial legislature may use s. 33 to override any Charter right, including s. 3 voting rights.
options.B: No. Section 3 democratic rights are expressly excluded from the scope of the s. 33 notwithstanding clause; a province cannot use s. 33 to override the right to vote. The disenfranchisement of prisoners must be justified under s. 1 of the Charter.
options.C: Yes. The right to vote under s. 3 is a political right, not a fundamental right, and is therefore subject to legislative override by the legislature of the province.
options.D: No, but the federal Parliament may use s. 33 to override s. 3 rights; only provinces are barred from doing so.

explanation: Section 33 of the Charter permits Parliament or a provincial legislature to invoke the notwithstanding clause in respect of a provision of the Charter that is included in s. 2 or ss. 7 to 15. Section 3 (democratic rights — right to vote and right to stand for election) is not included in the list of rights that can be overridden by s. 33. As confirmed by the Supreme Court of Canada in Sauve v Canada (Chief Electoral Officer), 2002 SCC 68, the right to vote under s. 3 is fundamental to Canadian democracy and cannot be overridden using s. 33. Any restriction on prisoner voting rights must be justified under s. 1 of the Charter.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Section 3 democratic rights are outside the scope of s. 33; the notwithstanding clause cannot be used to override the right to vote.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 3 democratic rights are expressly excluded from the scope of the s. 33 notwithstanding clause; a province cannot use s. 33 to override the right to vote. The disenfranchisement of prisoners must be justified under s. 1 of the Charter.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The right to vote under s. 3 is specifically protected as a democratic right; its character as political does not place it within the scope of s. 33 — quite the opposite, democratic rights are among those deliberately excluded from s. 33 override.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Neither Parliament nor provincial legislatures may invoke s. 33 to override s. 3 democratic rights; the exclusion applies to both levels of government.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.heading: s. 3 — Right to Vote and Right to Stand for Election
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 3, s. 33; Sauve v Canada (Chief Electoral Officer), 2002 SCC 68

subject: public_law
chapter_title: Charter of Rights and Freedoms — Section 1 and Key Rights
topic_heading: Charter Rights — Section 4 Duration of Legislative Bodies
subtopic_heading: s. 4 — Maximum Duration of Legislative Bodies
fact_pattern: Facing a severe economic crisis, a provincial government passes legislation extending the life of the existing provincial legislature for two additional years beyond the normal election date, arguing that holding an election during the crisis would be destabilizing. Opposition parties challenge the law as unconstitutional under s. 4 of the Charter. The government argues s. 33 of the Charter allows it to override s. 4.
call_of_question: Under s. 4 of the Charter, what is the maximum duration of a House of Commons or provincial legislative assembly, and is s. 4 subject to the s. 33 notwithstanding clause?

options.A: The maximum duration is five years from the date of the last general election; s. 4 may be overridden by a s. 33 declaration, allowing extensions beyond five years.
options.B: Under s. 4(1) of the Charter, no House of Commons or legislative assembly shall continue for longer than five years from the date fixed for the return of the writs at a general election. Section 4 is not subject to the s. 33 notwithstanding clause; the only permitted extension is under s. 4(2) in time of real or apprehended war, invasion, or insurrection, provided the extension is not opposed by more than one-third of the members of the legislature.
options.C: There is no maximum duration for provincial legislatures; only the House of Commons is subject to the five-year limit.
options.D: The maximum duration is four years; no extensions are permitted under any circumstances.

explanation: Section 4(1) of the Charter provides that no House of Commons and no legislative assembly shall continue for longer than five years from the date fixed for the return of the writs of a general election of its members. Section 4 is a democratic right expressly excluded from the scope of s. 33; unlike most Charter rights, s. 4 cannot be overridden by a notwithstanding clause declaration. The only constitutional basis for extending a legislature beyond five years is s. 4(2), which permits extension in time of real or apprehended war, invasion, or insurrection — and only if such continuation is not opposed by votes of more than one-third of the members. A general economic crisis does not meet the s. 4(2) threshold.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Section 4 is a democratic right excluded from the scope of s. 33; no notwithstanding clause declaration can extend the legislature beyond five years except under the narrow s. 4(2) exception.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 4(1) of the Charter, no House of Commons or legislative assembly shall continue for longer than five years; s. 4 is not subject to s. 33. The only permitted extension is under s. 4(2) in time of real or apprehended war, invasion, or insurrection, provided the extension is not opposed by more than one-third of members.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Section 4(1) expressly applies to both the House of Commons and every provincial legislative assembly; provinces are equally subject to the five-year maximum.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. The constitutional maximum is five years, not four; the five-year limit applies to both the House of Commons and provincial legislative assemblies under s. 4(1).

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.heading: s. 4 — Maximum Duration of Legislative Bodies
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 4(1), s. 4(2), s. 33
